perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall promptly return all of Owner's Confidential Information provided to it in tangible form, together with any and all copies and/or summaries, and shall destroy all of Owner's Confidential Information that is electronically stored; provided, however, that each Party's legal department may retain one copy of the Confidential Information in its file solely for the purpose of identifying information designated as "Confidential Information."
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall return all of Owner's Confidential Information provided to it in tangible form, together with any and all copies and/or summaries, and shall destroy all of Owner's Confidential Information that is electronically stored within 120 days; provided, however, that each Party's legal department may retain one copy of the Confidential Information in its file solely for the purpose of identifying information designated as "Confidential Information."
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly' to 'within 120 days' extends the return timeframe, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws requiring immediate action to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.108.030 states that injunctive relief may be granted to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. Delaying the return of confidential information for 120 days could be seen as allowing continued misappropriation, undermining the purpose of the UTSA, especially if it causes irreparable harm.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.030: Remedies for misappropriation—Damages. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.030 19.108.020 << 19.108.030 >> 19.108.040 PDF RCW 19.108.030 Remedies for misappropriation — Damages. (1) In addition to or in lieu of injunctive relief, a complainant may recover damages for the actual loss caused by misappropriation. A complainant also may recover for the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing damages for actual loss. (2) If wilful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (1). [ 1981 c 286 s 3 .]

perturbation.justification: The modification from 'promptly' to 'within 120 days' introduces a delay that could conflict with the need to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets, as indicated by the law explanation. RCW 19.108.030, as shown in both scraped snippets, addresses remedies for misappropriation, including injunctive relief and damages. Although the snippets don't explicitly define 'promptly,' the changed text introduces a specific timeframe that weakens the protection against potential misappropriation during the 120-day window, creating a contradiction because it permits a delay where immediate action might be required to prevent harm.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the breach or interpretation thereof, the parties shall, upon five days notice from either one to the other, submit themselves and the subject-matter of the dispute to arbitration in accordance with the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association by a single, disinterested arbitrator appointed in accordance with such Rules.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the breach or interpretation thereof, the parties shall, upon one day's notice from either one to the other, submit themselves and the subject-matter of the dispute to arbitration in accordance with the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association by a single, disinterested arbitrator appointed in accordance with such Rules.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the arbitration notice period to one day may be considered unreasonably short, potentially violating due process rights and contradicting the FAA's emphasis on fair arbitration procedures.
perturbation.law_explanation: 9 U.S.C. § 4 requires the court to order arbitration once it is satisfied that the making of the agreement for arbitration or the failure to comply therewith is not in issue. The FAA emphasizes the importance of a fair arbitration process. One day's notice might be seen as insufficient to prepare for arbitration, which violates principles of fairness.

Failure to arbitrate under agreement; petition to United States court having jurisdiction for order to compel arbitration; notice and service thereof; hearing and determination A party aggrieve
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 4 - Failure to arbitrate under agreement; petition to United States court having jurisdiction for order to compel arbitration; notice and service thereof; hearing and determination | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 4 - Failure to arbitrate under agreement; petition to United States court having jurisdiction for order to compel arbitration; notice and service thereof; hearing and determination U.S. Code Notes prev | next A party aggrieved by the alleged failure, neglect, or refusal of another to arbitrate under a written agreement for arbitration may petition any United States district court which, save for such agreement, would have jurisdiction under title 28, in a civil action or in admiralty of the subject matter of a suit arising out of the controversy between the parties, for an order directing that such arbitration proceed in the manner provided for in such agreement. Five days’ notice in writing of such application shall be served upon the party in default. Service thereof shall be made in the manner provided by the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. The court shall hear the parties, and upon being satisfied that the making of the agreement for arbitration or the failure to comply therewith is not in issue, the court shall make an order directing the parties to proceed to arbitration in accordance with the terms of the agreement. The hearing and proceedings, under such agreement, shall be within the district in which the petition for an order directing such arbitration is filed. If the making of the arbitration agreement or the failure, neglect, or refusal to perform the same be in issue, the court shall proceed summarily to the trial thereof. If no jury trial be demanded by the party alleged to be in default, or if the matter in dispute is within admiralty jurisdiction, the court shall hear and determine such issue. Where such an iss
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notice period for arbitration from five days to one day. This change directly contradicts the spirit of fair arbitration procedures as emphasized by the FAA. While 9 U.S.C. § 4, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, explicitly mentions 'Five days’ notice in writing,' it's specifically in the context of a petition to compel arbitration by a court. The original contract provided for five days' notice, aligning with this standard. Reducing this period to one day is arguably insufficient preparation time, undermining the fairness of the arbitration process and making the contractual term unenforceable due to its potential violation of due process.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the internal laws of the State of Washington, and, where such laws are preempted by the laws of the United States, by the internal laws of the United States, in each case without regard to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the internal laws of the State of Nevada, and, where such laws are preempted by the laws of the United States, by the internal laws of the United States, in each case without regard to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Nevada while the company Primus is in Washington could cause issues, particularly if the business is primarily conducted in WA. WA law has specific trade secrets rules, changing to Nevada might impact how the trade secrets are protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.108 outlines Washington's specific regulations for trade secret protection. By changing the governing law to Nevada, the agreement may inadvertently weaken the protections afforded to trade secrets under Washington law, where Primus Knowledge Solutions, Inc. is located and operates.

perturbation.justification: The modification changes the governing law from Washington to Nevada. The Law Explanation states that this change "may inadvertently weaken the protections afforded to trade secrets under Washington law." Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both identify RCW 19.108, Washington's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as providing specific regulations for trade secret protection. Since the company is in Washington, changing the governing law could impact the trade secrets protection, violating the requirement to protect the company's trade secrets under WA law. This constitutes a contradiction as the contract is now governed by a different set of laws, potentially weakening the trade secret protections afforded by Washington law, where the company is located.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice or other communication under this Agreement given by either party to the other party shall be deemed to be properly given if given in writing and delivered (i) by nationally recognized private courier (e.g., Federal Express) or (ii) by mail (return receipt requested), properly addressed and stamped with the required postage, to the recipient at the address identified in its signature block to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice or other communication under this Agreement given by either party to the other party shall be deemed to be properly given if given orally.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring only oral notice is insufficient for legal agreements. States have statutes regarding written notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.36.010, the Statute of Frauds, requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. Replacing the written notice requirement with an oral one makes the agreement unenforceable. Agreements that cannot be performed within one year must be in writing, which this agreement may violate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.36.010: Contracts, etc., void unless in writing. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.36 > Section 19.36.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.36.010 >> 19.36.020 PDF RCW 19.36.010 Contracts, etc., void unless in writing. In the following cases, specified in this section, any agreement, contract, and promise shall be void, unless such agreement, contract, or promise, or some note or memorandum thereof, be in writing, and signed by the party to be charged therewith, or by some person thereunto by him or her lawfully authorized, that is to say: (1) Every agreement that by its terms is not to be performed in one year from the making thereof; (2) every special promise to answer for the debt, default, or misdoings of another person; (3) every agreement, promise, or undertaking made upon consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; (4) every special promise made by an executor or administrator to answer damages out of his or her own estate; (5) an agreement authorizing or employing an agent or broker to sell or purchase real estate for compensation or a commission. [ 2011 c 336 s 540 ; 1905 c 58 s 1 ; RRS s 5825. Prior: Code 1881 s 2325; 1863 p 412 s 2 ; 1860 p 298 s 2 ; 1854 p 403 s 2 .]

Washington may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contai
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by allowing oral notice, directly contradicts RCW 19.36.010, which mandates that certain agreements, including those not performable within one year, must be in writing to be valid. The original text required written notice, ensuring compliance with this statute. The scraped law snippet explicitly states, 'any agreement, contract, and promise shall be void, unless such agreement, contract, or promise, or some note or memorandum thereof, be in writing, and signed by the party to be charged therewith,' as per RCW 19.36.010. By permitting oral notice, the changed text creates a legal contradiction, as it disregards the necessity of a written record for agreements potentially extending beyond one year, rendering the agreement potentially unenforceable.
